expanded_description: Intra-industry trade (IIT) refers to the simultaneous export and import of goods within the same industry, while inter-industry trade refers to the exchange of goods from different industries (e.g., exporting manufactured goods and importing agricultural products). Understanding the distinction between these two types of trade is fundamental to modern trade theory because they arise from different economic mechanisms and have different implications for welfare, adjustment costs, and trade policy. Inter-industry trade is explained by comparative advantage (Ricardian model) and factor endowments (Heckscher-Ohlin model) — countries export goods that use their abundant factors intensively and import goods that use their scarce factors. This type of trade tends to occur between countries with different factor endowments (developed vs developing countries) and generates adjustment costs that are high because resources must move across industries. Intra-industry trade is explained by economies of scale, product differentiation, and love of variety (Krugman model) — countries both export and import differentiated products within the same industry. This type of trade dominates between countries with similar factor endowments (developed countries) and generates lower adjustment costs because resources move within industries rather than across industries. The Grubel-Lloyd index measures the share of intra-industry trade, ranging from 0 (pure inter-industry trade) to 1 (pure intra-industry trade). Empirical evidence shows that intra-industry trade accounts for 60-80% of trade between developed economies and has been increasing over time due to globalization, supply chain fragmentation, and product differentiation. Understanding the distinction is essential for analyzing trade patterns, predicting the distributional effects of trade liberalization, designing adjustment assistance programs, and formulating trade policy.

core_concept.principle:
Inter-industry trade is the exchange of goods from different industries. A country exports products from one industry and imports products from a completely different industry. Examples: Germany exporting machinery to France and importing wine from France; the US exporting aircraft to Brazil and importing coffee from Brazil; China exporting electronics to the US and importing soybeans from the US. Inter-industry trade is explained by differences between countries — differences in technology (Ricardian model), differences in factor endowments (Heckscher-Ohlin model), or differences in productivity. The pattern of inter-industry trade is determined by comparative advantage: each country exports goods in which it has a lower opportunity cost.

Intra-industry trade is the simultaneous export and import of goods within the same industry. A country exports some varieties of a product and imports other varieties of the same product. Examples: Germany exporting BMWs to France and importing Renaults from France; the US exporting iPhones (designed in US, assembled in China) and importing Samsung phones from Korea; Japan exporting Toyota cars to the US and importing Ford cars from the US. Intra-industry trade is explained by similarities between countries — similar factor endowments, similar technology, similar preferences — combined with economies of scale, product differentiation, and love of variety (Krugman model). The pattern of intra-industry trade is determined by increasing returns and consumer preferences for variety, not by comparative advantage.

The Grubel-Lloyd (GL) index measures the share of intra-industry trade for an industry or country: $GL_i = 1 - \frac{|X_i - M_i|}{X_i + M_i}$, where $X_i$ is exports and $M_i$ is imports in industry i. The index ranges from 0 (no intra-industry trade, all trade is inter-industry: either $X_i=0$ or $M_i=0$) to 1 (perfect intra-industry trade: $X_i = M_i$). A GL index of 0.6 means 60% of trade in that industry is intra-industry and 40% is inter-industry. For a country overall, the weighted average GL index is calculated using total trade as weights.

Key differences: (1) Source of gains: Inter-industry trade gains come from exploiting comparative advantage (more efficient resource allocation); intra-industry trade gains come from economies of scale and increased product variety. (2) Adjustment costs: Inter-industry trade requires resources to move across industries (e.g., from agriculture to manufacturing), generating high adjustment costs; intra-industry trade requires resources to move within industries (e.g., from Ford to Toyota), generating lower adjustment costs. (3) Distributional effects: Inter-industry trade benefits owners of abundant factors and harms owners of scarce factors (Stolper-Samuelson); intra-industry trade benefits all workers in an industry (though some firms may lose) and all consumers through variety. (4) Trade partners: Inter-industry trade dominates between dissimilar countries (North-South trade); intra-industry trade dominates between similar countries (North-North trade). (5) Policy implications: Inter-industry trade may justify protection for scarce factors (political economy); intra-industry trade faces less political opposition because losers are fewer and adjustment is easier.
core_concept.explanation:
Consider two countries: the United States (capital-abundant) and Mexico (labor-abundant). Trade between them is primarily inter-industry. The US exports capital-intensive goods — aircraft, machinery, chemicals, agricultural products (mechanized farming). Mexico exports labor-intensive goods — textiles, apparel, assembled electronics, fruits and vegetables. A US worker displaced from the textile industry (import-competing) cannot easily move to the aircraft industry — skills differ, locations differ, capital differs. Adjustment costs are high. The political opposition to NAFTA from US textile workers and from Mexican farmers (who faced competition from US agriculture) reflects the inter-industry nature of US-Mexico trade.

Now consider trade between the United States and Germany. Both are capital-abundant, developed economies. Trade between them is primarily intra-industry. The US exports Ford cars to Germany and imports BMWs; exports Boeing aircraft and imports Airbus; exports Caterpillar construction equipment and imports Liebherr; exports Microsoft software and imports SAP software. A US auto worker displaced from Ford (because BMW gained market share) can likely move to a Toyota or Honda plant in the US — same industry, similar skills, often same region. Adjustment costs are lower. Political opposition to US-EU trade agreements is much weaker than opposition to NAFTA or US-China trade because the adjustment is intra-industry.

The Grubel-Lloyd index quantifies this difference. For US-Mexico trade in automobiles, exports might be \$20 billion (US exports to Mexico) and imports \$60 billion (US imports from Mexico). GL = 1 - |20-60|/80 = 1 - 40/80 = 0.5 — half intra-industry, half inter-industry. For US-Germany auto trade, exports \$50 billion, imports \$50 billion, GL = 1 - 0/100 = 1.0 — pure intra-industry trade.

Why does intra-industry trade matter for policy? Because adjustment costs determine political feasibility. When the US signed the Auto Pact with Canada (1965) and later CUSFTA (1989), the adjustment was relatively smooth — it was intra-industry trade. When the US signed NAFTA with Mexico (1994), the adjustment was much harder — inter-industry trade required workers to move from textiles and apparel to other sectors, often requiring retraining and relocation. This explains why trade adjustment assistance (TAA) programs are more generous for workers displaced by inter-industry trade and why trade agreements with developing countries face more political opposition than agreements with other developed countries.

Example: China's WTO accession (2001) dramatically increased US-China trade. This trade is primarily inter-industry — the US exports capital-intensive goods (aircraft, machinery, chemicals, soybeans) and imports labor-intensive goods (textiles, furniture, electronics assembly). The adjustment costs were substantial: US manufacturing employment declined, especially in labor-intensive industries, and workers faced long-term wage scarring. The political backlash against trade with China was intense. In contrast, US trade with Canada and Europe remains largely intra-industry and generates much less political opposition.

image.alt: Overview of intra-industry vs inter-industry trade showing examples, Grubel-Lloyd index, and adjustment cost differences
image.caption: Figure 7.3.1: Intra-industry vs inter-industry trade. Inter-industry trade exchanges different products (cars for wine); intra-industry trade exchanges differentiated products (BMW for Renault). The Grubel-Lloyd index measures the share of intra-industry trade from 0 to 1.

sections.introduction: Intra-industry trade (IIT) is the simultaneous export and import of goods belonging to the same industry classification. Measuring IIT requires industry-level trade data and an index that captures the extent to which exports and imports overlap within an industry. The Grubel-Lloyd index is the standard measure, but its interpretation depends on the level of industry aggregation — more detailed classifications yield lower IIT measures because products that appear similar at a broad level are actually different at a detailed level.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.3.2: Measuring intra-industry trade requires industry-level export and import data. The Grubel-Lloyd index ranges from 0 (pure inter-industry trade) to 1 (pure intra-industry trade).

key_concept.definition:
The Grubel-Lloyd (GL) index for industry i is: $GL_i = 1 - \frac{|X_i - M_i|}{X_i + M_i}$, where $X_i$ is exports and $M_i$ is imports in industry i. The index has the following properties: (1) $0 \leq GL_i \leq 1$. (2) $GL_i = 0$ when either $X_i = 0$ or $M_i = 0$ (pure inter-industry trade — all trade is one-way). (3) $GL_i = 1$ when $X_i = M_i$ (pure intra-industry trade — exports exactly equal imports). (4) $GL_i = 0.5$ when the smaller flow is half the larger flow (e.g., $X_i = 100$, $M_i = 50$, then $GL = 1 - 50/150 = 0.667$).

For a country overall, the weighted average GL index is: $GL_{overall} = \frac{\sum_i (X_i + M_i) \cdot GL_i}{\sum_i (X_i + M_i)} = 1 - \frac{\sum_i |X_i - M_i|}{\sum_i (X_i + M_i)}$. This measures the share of total trade that is intra-industry. A value of 0.7 means 70% of trade is intra-industry, 30% inter-industry.

The level of industry aggregation matters. At a broad level (e.g., 'transport equipment'), IIT appears high because cars, trucks, and aircraft are grouped together. At a detailed level (e.g., 'passenger vehicles with engine displacement 1500-2000cc'), IIT is lower because products are more narrowly defined. Economists typically use 3- or 4-digit SITC or HS codes to balance detail and data availability. The 'smooth adjustment hypothesis' predicts that industries with higher GL indices experience lower adjustment costs from trade liberalization because displaced workers can find jobs within the same industry.
key_concept.context: The Grubel-Lloyd index was introduced by Herbert Grubel and Peter Lloyd in their 1975 book 'Intra-Industry Trade: The Theory and Measurement of International Trade in Differentiated Products.' They documented that a large share of trade between developed countries was two-way trade in similar products, which could not be explained by comparative advantage. Their index remains the standard measure of IIT. Subsequent research by Aquino (1978) and Greenaway and Milner (1983) refined the measurement to account for trade imbalances and aggregation bias. The 'smooth adjustment hypothesis' — that IIT reduces adjustment costs — was developed by Balassa (1966) and has been extensively tested. Empirical studies generally confirm that higher GL indices are associated with lower adjustment costs, though the effect varies across industries and countries.

image.alt: Diagram showing Grubel-Lloyd index formula and calculation examples
image.caption: Figure 7.3.3: The Grubel-Lloyd index formula and examples for different trade patterns. GL=1 when exports equal imports; GL=0 when trade is one-way; intermediate values reflect partial intra-industry trade.

categories.description: Step-by-step calculation of GL indices for industries and countries.

examples.explanation:
For an industry with exports X and imports M, compute GL = 1 - |X-M|/(X+M).

Example: Automobiles: X = \$80 billion, M = \$60 billion. GL = 1 - |80-60|/140 = 1 - 20/140 = 1 - 0.1429 = 0.8571. This means 85.7% of automobile trade is intra-industry, 14.3% is inter-industry.

Weighted average of industry GL indices using total trade (X+M) as weights: $GL_{overall} = \frac{\sum (X_i+M_i) \cdot GL_i}{\sum (X_i+M_i)}$.

Example: Two industries. Industry A: X=100, M=100, trade=200, GL=1. Industry B: X=200, M=0, trade=200, GL=0. Overall GL = (200×1 + 200×0)/400 = 200/400 = 0.5. Half of total trade is intra-industry, half inter-industry.

When a country has an overall trade surplus or deficit, the standard GL index can be biased. Adjusted measures (Aquino index) normalize exports and imports to eliminate the effect of aggregate imbalance. However, for most developed countries with roughly balanced trade, the standard GL index works well.

Example: Country has overall trade surplus of \$100 billion. This surplus must appear as net exports in some industries, biasing GL indices downward for those industries. The adjusted index reallocates the surplus proportionally across industries to remove the bias.

categories.description: How the level of industry detail affects measured IIT.

examples.explanation:
At broad industry levels (1-digit SITC), many different products are grouped together, so IIT appears high because trade in different products within the same broad category counts as IIT. At narrow levels (5-digit SITC or 10-digit HS), products are more homogeneous, so IIT is lower. Economists must choose a classification level appropriate for the question being studied.

Example: 'Transport equipment' (broad) includes cars, trucks, buses, aircraft, ships, and railway equipment. US exports aircraft to France and imports cars from France — this counts as IIT at the broad level but is actually inter-industry trade at the detailed level. At the detailed level, 'passenger cars' vs 'aircraft' are different industries.

examples.process: The GL index and product differentiation
examples.explanation:
The GL index is higher for industries with more product differentiation (automobiles, electronics, pharmaceuticals) and lower for industries with homogeneous products (steel, cement, basic chemicals). This pattern supports the Krugman model's prediction that differentiation drives IIT.

Example: Automobiles GL ≈ 0.8-0.9; Steel GL ≈ 0.2-0.3. The difference reflects that cars are highly differentiated (brand, style, features) while steel is a commodity (price is the main differentiator).

Horizontal IIT refers to trade in products of similar quality (differentiated by attributes, not quality). Vertical IIT refers to trade in products of different quality levels (e.g., high-quality German cars for low-quality Korean cars). Vertical IIT may be explained by factor endowments (skill differences) rather than scale economies. The GL index does not distinguish between horizontal and vertical IIT without additional quality data.

Example: US exports high-end luxury cars (Lexus, BMW made in US) and imports economy cars (Kia, Hyundai) from Korea — this is vertical IIT. US and Germany exchange BMW and Mercedes (both high-end) — horizontal IIT.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Grubel-Lloyd Indices

image.alt: Example calculating Grubel-Lloyd indices for multiple industries
image.caption: Figure 7.3.4: Computing GL indices for five industries and the overall weighted average.

worked_examples.problem: A country has the following trade data for five industries. Industry 1: X=100, M=100. Industry 2: X=150, M=50. Industry 3: X=80, M=120. Industry 4: X=200, M=0. Industry 5: X=30, M=30. (a) Calculate GL index for each industry. (b) Calculate the overall GL index (weighted average). (c) What share of total trade is intra-industry? (d) Which industries have pure inter-industry trade? Which have pure intra-industry trade?

step_by_step.explanation: Overall GL = weighted average using total trade as weights: $GL = \frac{\sum (X_i+M_i) GL_i}{\sum (X_i+M_i)}$.
The overall GL index directly gives the share of total trade that is intra-industry.
step_by_step.calculation: 60.5% of total trade is intra-industry; 39.5% is inter-industry.

step_by_step.explanation: Pure intra-industry trade (GL=1): Industries 1 and 5 (exports equal imports). Pure inter-industry trade (GL=0): Industry 4 (exports only, no imports). Industries 2 and 3 have mixed patterns.
step_by_step.calculation: Pure IIT: Industries 1 and 5. Pure inter-industry: Industry 4.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Ind1:1.00, Ind2:0.50, Ind3:0.80, Ind4:0.00, Ind5:1.00; (b) Overall GL = 0.605; (c) 60.5% intra-industry; (d) Pure IIT: Industries 1 and 5; Pure inter-industry: Industry 4.
worked_examples.concept_applied: The Grubel-Lloyd index measures intra-industry trade intensity. A value of 1 indicates perfect two-way trade; 0 indicates one-way trade. The overall index is the weighted average across industries, reflecting the share of total trade that is intra-industry.

worked_examples.title: Comparing IIT Across Country Pairs

image.alt: Example comparing IIT across different country pairs
image.caption: Figure 7.3.5: Comparing intra-industry trade shares for different country pairs — high between similar countries, low between dissimilar countries.

worked_examples.problem: Country A (developed, capital-abundant) trades with three partners. Country B (developed, capital-abundant). Country C (developing, labor-abundant). Country D (developing, resource-abundant). Trade data (\$ billion) for the manufacturing sector. With B: X=500, M=500. With C: X=600, M=200. With D: X=300, M=300. (a) Calculate GL index for each country pair. (b) Explain why A-B trade has the highest IIT. (c) Explain why A-C trade has the lowest IIT. (d) What does this pattern tell us about the determinants of intra-industry trade?

step_by_step.explanation: Apply GL formula to each country pair.

step_by_step.step: Part (b): Why A-B has highest IIT
step_by_step.explanation: Countries A and B are both developed, capital-abundant economies with similar factor endowments, technology, and income levels. Trade between them is dominated by differentiated products (intra-industry) explained by economies of scale and love of variety. They exchange similar products (cars for cars, electronics for electronics).
step_by_step.calculation: Similar endowments → high IIT.

step_by_step.step: Part (c): Why A-C has lower IIT
step_by_step.explanation: Country C is developing and labor-abundant. A is capital-abundant. Trade is driven by factor endowment differences (Heckscher-Ohlin): A exports capital-intensive goods to C, C exports labor-intensive goods to A. This is inter-industry trade, not intra-industry. The GL index of 0.5 reflects mixed patterns — some IIT in differentiated products, but significant inter-industry trade.
step_by_step.calculation: Dissimilar endowments → lower IIT.

step_by_step.explanation: The pattern shows that intra-industry trade is highest between countries with similar factor endowments (developed-developed). It can also be high between developed and resource-abundant countries if trade involves differentiated products (A-D shows GL=1, possibly reflecting differentiated manufactured goods exchanged for resources). The key determinants of IIT are: (1) Similarity in factor endowments (developed-developed pairs). (2) Similarity in income per capita (higher incomes → more demand for differentiated products). (3) Trade costs (lower costs increase IIT). (4) Product differentiation (more differentiation → more IIT).
step_by_step.calculation: IIT is highest between similar economies and in industries with product differentiation.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) A-B: 1.00, A-C: 0.50, A-D: 1.00; (b) A and B have similar factor endowments (both developed), so trade is intra-industry; (c) A and C have different factor endowments (developed vs developing), so trade is inter-industry; (d) IIT is highest between countries with similar endowments, income levels, and in industries with product differentiation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Intra-industry trade is highest between countries with similar factor endowments and income levels (developed-developed pairs). Inter-industry trade dominates between dissimilar countries (developed-developing pairs). The Grubel-Lloyd index captures this pattern.

image.caption: Figure 7.3.6: Summary of measuring intra-industry trade — GL index formula, calculation examples, aggregation issues, and interpretation guidelines.

sections.introduction: Several factors determine the level of intra-industry trade between countries and across industries. Country-level determinants include similarity in factor endowments, similarity in income per capita, geographic proximity, and trade agreement membership. Industry-level determinants include product differentiation, economies of scale, and the presence of multinational corporations and global supply chains. Understanding these determinants helps explain why IIT is high between developed countries and low between developed and developing countries.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to determinants of intra-industry trade
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.3.7: Determinants of intra-industry trade include country similarity (endowments, income), industry characteristics (differentiation, scale economies), and trade costs (distance, agreements).

key_concept.definition:
Country-level determinants of intra-industry trade: (1) Factor endowment similarity — countries with similar capital-labor ratios have higher IIT because they are not driven to specialize according to comparative advantage. The Helpman-Krugman model predicts that IIT increases as countries become more similar in factor endowments. (2) Income per capita similarity — countries with similar income levels have similar demand patterns (Linder hypothesis), both demanding differentiated products, increasing IIT. (3) Geographic proximity — lower trade costs (distance, tariffs, transport costs) increase IIT because they make it easier to exchange differentiated products. (4) Common language and colonial ties — reduce transaction costs and increase IIT. (5) Trade agreements — reduce tariffs and non-tariff barriers, increasing IIT. (6) Country size — larger countries have more varieties to export and import, increasing IIT.

Industry-level determinants: (1) Product differentiation — industries with more differentiated products (automobiles, electronics, pharmaceuticals) have higher IIT than industries with homogeneous products (steel, cement, basic chemicals). (2) Economies of scale — industries with larger fixed costs (aircraft, semiconductors) have higher IIT because firms need global markets to achieve scale. (3) Multinational corporations and supply chains — vertical specialization (producing components in one country, assembling in another) creates intra-firm trade that appears as IIT in trade statistics. (4) Research and development intensity — high-R&D industries tend to have more product differentiation and higher IIT. (5) Advertising intensity — high-advertising industries (consumer goods) have more brand differentiation and higher IIT.

The Linder hypothesis (1961) states that countries with similar per capita incomes have similar demand structures and therefore trade more in manufactured goods. This implies higher IIT between countries with similar incomes. Empirical evidence broadly supports the Linder hypothesis for differentiated products.
key_concept.context: The determinants of intra-industry trade have been extensively studied empirically. Helpman (1981) and Helpman and Krugman (1985) developed theoretical models predicting that IIT increases with country similarity. Hummels and Levinsohn (1995) found that similarity in factor endowments is a strong predictor of IIT. The Linder hypothesis (1961) — that countries with similar incomes trade more — has been tested and generally confirmed for differentiated goods but not for homogeneous goods. Bergstrand (1990) found that IIT is higher between countries with similar GDP per capita, similar GDP, and geographic proximity. Industry-level studies (Greenaway, Hine, Milner, 1995) found that IIT is higher in industries with high product differentiation, high R&D intensity, and high economies of scale. The rise of global value chains has increased measured IIT because components and finished goods flow in both directions within the same industry (e.g., auto parts shipped from US to Mexico, assembled cars shipped back).

categories.description: Factors at the country-pair level that affect IIT.

examples.explanation:
Countries with similar capital-labor ratios have higher IIT. The Helpman-Krugman model shows that as countries become more similar, the share of intra-industry trade increases. When countries are very different, trade is dominated by inter-industry trade based on comparative advantage.

Example: US-Canada (similar capital-labor ratios) have IIT share >0.8. US-Mexico (different ratios) have IIT share <0.4.

Countries with similar per capita incomes have similar demand patterns — both demand differentiated products. Consumers in high-income countries demand quality and variety; consumers in low-income countries demand basic products. Similar-income countries trade more differentiated products with each other.

Example: Germany and France (similar high incomes) have high IIT. Germany and India (different incomes) have low IIT.

Lower trade costs (shorter distance, lower tariffs, better infrastructure) increase IIT because differentiated products are sensitive to trade costs — consumers are willing to pay for variety but not infinite transport costs. Regional trade agreements (EU, NAFTA, ASEAN) significantly increase IIT among members.

Example: US-Canada IIT increased dramatically after the 1989 Canada-US Free Trade Agreement and 1994 NAFTA. Distance matters: US-UK IIT is lower than US-Canada IIT despite similar incomes because the Atlantic Ocean increases trade costs.

categories.description: Factors within industries that affect IIT.

examples.explanation:
Industries with high product differentiation (many varieties, brands, quality levels) have higher IIT because there is scope for two-way trade in different varieties. Homogeneous products (commodities) have low or zero IIT.

Example: Automobiles (high differentiation) have GL ≈ 0.8-0.9. Cement (low differentiation) has GL ≈ 0.0-0.1.

Industries with large fixed costs (economies of scale) have higher IIT because firms need to serve global markets to achieve efficient scale. Each country cannot produce all varieties efficiently, so they specialize in different varieties and trade.

Example: Aircraft manufacturing (very high fixed costs, \$10-15 billion per new model) has high IIT between US and Europe (Boeing vs Airbus). Each region produces different models and exports to the other.

examples.process: Global value chains and vertical specialization
examples.explanation:
When production is fragmented across countries (components made in one country, assembled in another, final product exported back), trade statistics show two-way flows within the same industry. A country may export auto parts and import assembled cars — both in 'automotive' industry, creating IIT that reflects supply chains, not product differentiation.

Example: US exports auto parts to Mexico, Mexico assembles cars and exports them back to the US. This appears as IIT in the automotive industry but is actually vertical specialization. The GL index does not distinguish between horizontal IIT (differentiated final goods) and vertical IIT (supply chain trade).

worked_examples.title: Predicting IIT Levels Based on Country Characteristics

image.alt: Example predicting IIT levels from country characteristics
image.caption: Figure 7.3.8: Predicting intra-industry trade levels based on factor endowment similarity, income similarity, distance, and industry differentiation.

worked_examples.problem: Rank the following country pairs from highest to lowest expected intra-industry trade share. Explain your ranking. Pairs: (1) US-Canada, (2) US-Mexico, (3) US-Japan, (4) US-Vietnam, (5) Germany-France, (6) Germany-Poland.

step_by_step.explanation: IIT is highest when countries have similar factor endowments (both developed), similar incomes, geographic proximity, and when industries are differentiated.
step_by_step.calculation: Developed-developed pairs: US-Canada, US-Japan, Germany-France, Germany-Poland (Poland is developing but closer to Germany than Vietnam to US).

step_by_step.explanation: Highest IIT: Germany-France (both EU, similar incomes, similar endowments, very close, EU single market). Next: US-Canada (similar incomes, similar endowments, close, USMCA). Next: Germany-Poland (similar endowments? Poland is less developed but close, EU member, significant supply chain integration). Next: US-Japan (both developed but distant, less IIT than Canada). Next: US-Mexico (different endowments, but USMCA and proximity increase IIT somewhat). Lowest: US-Vietnam (very different endowments, distant, no trade agreement, low IIT).
step_by_step.calculation:
1. Germany-France (GL ≈ 0.85-0.95)
2. US-Canada (GL ≈ 0.80-0.90)
3. Germany-Poland (GL ≈ 0.60-0.75)
4. US-Japan (GL ≈ 0.55-0.70)
5. US-Mexico (GL ≈ 0.35-0.50)
6. US-Vietnam (GL ≈ 0.15-0.30)

step_by_step.explanation: Germany-France: both high-income, EU members, close, similar endowments → very high IIT. US-Canada: similar incomes, USMCA, close → high IIT. Germany-Poland: EU membership and proximity raise IIT despite income differences. US-Japan: both developed but distant, moderate IIT. US-Mexico: income and endowment differences reduce IIT, but USMCA and proximity help. US-Vietnam: large differences in endowments, income, distance → low IIT.
step_by_step.calculation: Proximity and similarity drive IIT.

worked_examples.final_answer: Highest to lowest IIT: Germany-France > US-Canada > Germany-Poland > US-Japan > US-Mexico > US-Vietnam. IIT is driven by similarity in endowments, income, geographic proximity, and trade agreements.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Intra-industry trade is highest between countries that are similar (developed-developed, similar income, close geography) and lowest between dissimilar countries (developed-developing, distant). Trade agreements and regional integration increase IIT.

worked_examples.title: Industry-Level IIT Variation

image.alt: Example showing industry-level variation in IIT
image.caption: Figure 7.3.9: Intra-industry trade varies significantly across industries based on product differentiation, scale economies, and vertical specialization.

worked_examples.problem: For a developed country (e.g., Germany), rank the following industries from highest to lowest expected IIT. Industries: Automobiles, Steel, Pharmaceuticals, Textiles/Apparel, Electronics, Cement, Chemicals (specialty), Chemicals (basic). Explain your ranking based on product differentiation, economies of scale, and vertical specialization.

step_by_step.explanation: High IIT industries: high product differentiation, high economies of scale, high R&D intensity, vertical supply chains. Low IIT industries: homogeneous products, low scale economies, commodity-like.
step_by_step.calculation: Differentiation: Autos (high), Pharma (high), Electronics (high), Specialty Chemicals (medium), Textiles (medium), Steel (low), Basic Chemicals (low), Cement (very low).

step_by_step.explanation: Highest IIT: Pharmaceuticals (differentiated, patents, high R&D) and Automobiles (differentiated, brands, global supply chains). Next: Electronics (differentiated, global value chains). Next: Specialty Chemicals (differentiated). Next: Textiles/Apparel (differentiated but lower value, some vertical specialization). Next: Steel (commodity, low differentiation). Next: Basic Chemicals (commodity). Lowest: Cement (very heavy, low value-to-weight, produced locally, minimal trade).
step_by_step.calculation:
1. Pharmaceuticals (GL ≈ 0.85-0.95)
2. Automobiles (GL ≈ 0.80-0.90)
3. Electronics (GL ≈ 0.70-0.85)
4. Specialty Chemicals (GL ≈ 0.60-0.75)
5. Textiles/Apparel (GL ≈ 0.50-0.65)
6. Steel (GL ≈ 0.20-0.35)
7. Basic Chemicals (GL ≈ 0.15-0.30)
8. Cement (GL ≈ 0.00-0.10)

step_by_step.explanation: Textiles/apparel has moderate IIT because of vertical specialization (developed countries design and market, developing countries produce). Steel has low IIT because it is a commodity, but specialty steels have higher IIT. Cement has extremely low IIT because transport costs are high relative to product value (cement is heavy, low value), so trade is mostly local.
step_by_step.calculation: Transport costs and differentiation drive industry IIT variation.

worked_examples.final_answer: Highest to lowest IIT: Pharmaceuticals > Automobiles > Electronics > Specialty Chemicals > Textiles/Apparel > Steel > Basic Chemicals > Cement. IIT is higher in differentiated, high-R&D industries with scale economies and lower in homogeneous, commodity industries.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Intra-industry trade varies significantly across industries. Differentiated products (automobiles, pharmaceuticals, electronics) have high IIT. Homogeneous products (cement, basic steel, basic chemicals) have low IIT. Vertical specialization (supply chains) increases measured IIT for industries like electronics and textiles.

image.caption: Figure 7.3.10: Summary of determinants of intra-industry trade — country similarity (endowments, income, proximity), industry characteristics (differentiation, scale, R&D), and trade policy (agreements, barriers).

sections.introduction: The distinction between intra-industry and inter-industry trade has profound implications for the adjustment costs associated with trade liberalization and for trade policy design. Inter-industry trade requires resources to move across industries, which is costly — workers need new skills, capital must be redeployed or scrapped, and regional economies may be disrupted. Intra-industry trade requires resources to move within industries, which is much less costly — skills are transferable, capital can be repurposed, and regional impacts are smaller. This difference explains why trade agreements between similar countries face less political opposition than agreements between dissimilar countries and why trade adjustment assistance is targeted at workers displaced by inter-industry trade.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to adjustment costs and policy implications
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.3.11: Intra-industry trade generates lower adjustment costs than inter-industry trade because resources move within industries rather than across industries. This affects political feasibility and policy design.

key_concept.definition:
The smooth adjustment hypothesis (SAH) states that industries with higher intra-industry trade experience lower adjustment costs following trade liberalization. The intuition: when trade patterns shift, workers and capital in industries with high IIT can move between firms within the same industry (e.g., from Ford to Toyota), requiring minimal retraining and no industry-specific capital loss. In industries with low IIT (inter-industry trade), displaced workers must move to different industries (e.g., from textiles to electronics), requiring significant retraining, relocation, and often involving capital scrapping.

Empirical evidence supports the SAH: (1) Worker displacement studies show that workers in high-IIT industries find new jobs faster and with smaller wage losses than workers in low-IIT industries. (2) Regional studies show that regions specialized in high-IIT industries recover faster from trade shocks. (3) Firm-level studies show that capital in high-IIT industries is more easily redeployed.

Policy implications: (1) Trade agreements between similar countries (high IIT) face less political opposition and require less adjustment assistance. (2) Trade agreements between dissimilar countries (low IIT) generate more opposition and require more generous adjustment assistance. (3) Trade Adjustment Assistance (TAA) programs should be targeted at workers in low-IIT industries where adjustment costs are highest. (4) Retraining programs should focus on skills transferable across industries for workers displaced by inter-industry trade. (5) Regional development policies may be needed for regions specialized in low-IIT industries that face permanent decline.

The smooth adjustment hypothesis also implies that the political economy of trade differs across country pairs. North-North trade (high IIT) generates diffuse benefits (variety, lower prices) and concentrated but manageable costs (intra-industry adjustment). North-South trade (low IIT) generates clear winners (export industries) and losers (import-competing industries) with high adjustment costs, leading to more intense political conflict.
key_concept.context: The smooth adjustment hypothesis was proposed by Balassa (1966) and formalized by Krugman (1981). Empirical tests have generally confirmed the hypothesis. Studies of US trade adjustment (Grossman and Levinsohn, 1989) found that workers in high-IIT industries experienced smaller wage declines after import competition. Studies of EU integration (Brülhart, 2009) found that regions with higher IIT had faster employment recovery. Studies of NAFTA (Hakobyan and McLaren, 2016) found that workers in low-IIT industries (textiles, apparel) experienced persistent wage losses, while workers in high-IIT industries (autos, electronics) adjusted more quickly. The hypothesis has been used to justify differential treatment in TAA programs — the US TAA program provides more generous benefits to workers displaced by trade in industries with low IIT, though the program does not explicitly use the GL index in eligibility determination.

image.alt: Diagram showing smooth adjustment hypothesis and adjustment cost differences
image.caption: Figure 7.3.12: The smooth adjustment hypothesis — high intra-industry trade (GL close to 1) generates low adjustment costs because resources move within industries. Low IIT (GL close to 0) generates high adjustment costs because resources move across industries.

categories.description: Why inter-industry trade generates higher adjustment costs than intra-industry trade.

examples.explanation:
Workers displaced from a declining industry need to find jobs in expanding industries. With intra-industry trade, both declining and expanding firms are in the same industry — skills are similar, so retraining requirements are minimal. With inter-industry trade, workers must move to different industries, often requiring significant retraining or accepting wage cuts. Human capital specific to the declining industry is lost (skill scarring).

Example: An auto worker displaced from Ford (intra-industry adjustment) can move to Toyota or Honda with minimal retraining — skills (assembly, welding, quality control) are directly transferable. A textile worker displaced (inter-industry adjustment) trying to move to healthcare may need years of retraining and may never regain previous wages.

Capital specific to an industry (factories, machinery, specialized equipment) can be redeployed within the same industry but is often worthless in other industries. With intra-industry trade, capital can be sold to or repurposed by expanding firms within the industry. With inter-industry trade, capital may need to be scrapped, representing a sunk cost loss. This loss reduces the wealth of capital owners and may have broader financial consequences.

Example: A textile mill (specific capital for weaving) cannot be converted into a semiconductor fab. The capital is scrapped, and the owner takes a loss. An auto assembly plant can be converted to produce different models or sold to another automaker with minimal loss.

Industries often concentrate geographically (e.g., textiles in the US Southeast, autos in the Midwest). When an industry declines, the entire region may suffer. Intra-industry trade involves both export and import-competing firms in the same region, so some firms expand while others contract, partially offsetting regional employment effects. Inter-industry trade involves whole industries declining in a region, with no offsetting expansion in the same industry, leading to persistent regional decline.

Example: In the US Midwest, auto industry decline was partially offset by expansion of foreign-owned auto plants (Toyota, Honda, Hyundai) in the same region — intra-industry adjustment. In the US Southeast, textile decline had no offsetting expansion in textiles — inter-industry adjustment required workers to move to different industries, often requiring out-migration from the region.

categories.description: Studies testing the smooth adjustment hypothesis.

examples.explanation:
Research finds that workers displaced from industries with high IIT have shorter unemployment spells and smaller wage losses than workers from low-IIT industries. The effect persists even after controlling for worker characteristics (age, education, tenure).

Example: A study of US manufacturing workers found that those in industries with GL > 0.7 experienced wage losses of 5-10% after displacement, while those in industries with GL < 0.3 experienced wage losses of 20-30%.

Regions specialized in high-IIT industries recover faster from trade shocks. Employment in these regions returns to pre-shock levels within 2-5 years. Regions specialized in low-IIT industries may experience permanent employment declines and persistent wage depression.

Example: Following NAFTA, US regions with high IIT in autos (Michigan, Ohio, Indiana) saw employment recover within 5 years. Regions with low IIT in textiles (North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia) saw permanent employment declines in manufacturing and persistent wage depression even after 15 years.

Countries that trade more with similar partners (high IIT) have lower adjustment costs and more stable political support for trade liberalization. Countries that trade more with dissimilar partners (low IIT) experience more trade-related political conflict and are more likely to impose protectionist measures.

Example: European Union members (high IIT among themselves) have stable support for free trade. The US has higher political opposition to trade with China and Mexico (low IIT) than to trade with Canada and Europe (high IIT).

categories.description: How the IIT/inter-industry distinction affects trade policy design.

examples.explanation:
TAA programs provide income support, retraining, and relocation assistance to workers displaced by trade. The smooth adjustment hypothesis suggests that TAA should be targeted at workers in low-IIT industries where adjustment costs are highest. Some countries (US, Canada) have used industry IIT levels to determine eligibility or benefit levels.

Example: The US TAA program provides up to 130 weeks of income support and \$20,000 for retraining. Workers in low-IIT industries (textiles, apparel, furniture) are more likely to qualify and use benefits than workers in high-IIT industries (autos, electronics).

When designing trade agreements, policymakers can sequence liberalization to manage adjustment costs. Liberalizing trade in high-IIT industries first (where adjustment is easier) builds political support for further liberalization. Liberalizing low-IIT industries later, with longer phase-in periods and generous adjustment assistance, can reduce opposition.

Example: NAFTA phased out tariffs on autos (higher IIT) more quickly than tariffs on textiles and apparel (lower IIT). This sequencing recognized the higher adjustment costs in low-IIT industries.

The political feasibility of trade agreements depends on whether losers can be compensated. Inter-industry trade creates concentrated losses (specific industries, specific regions) that are visible and politically powerful. Intra-industry trade creates diffuse losses (individual firms, not whole industries) that are less politically potent. Compensation is easier and more effective for inter-industry trade losses because losers are identifiable and can be targeted.

Example: The US government provided \$12 billion in aid to farmers (inter-industry trade losers from China trade) during the US-China trade war. Farmers are geographically concentrated and politically influential. There was no comparable aid for workers displaced by intra-industry trade because losses were less concentrated.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Adjustment Costs Across Industries

image.alt: Example comparing adjustment costs across industries with different GL indices
image.caption: Figure 7.3.13: Computing expected adjustment costs based on GL index and industry characteristics.

worked_examples.problem: Three industries have GL indices: Industry A (GL=0.9), Industry B (GL=0.5), Industry C (GL=0.1). All face a 20% increase in imports due to trade liberalization. (a) Rank the industries by expected adjustment costs (highest to lowest). (b) Estimate the expected wage loss for displaced workers in each industry (as a percentage of pre-displacement wages). (c) What policy interventions would be appropriate for each industry? (d) If the government has limited TAA funds, which industry should receive priority?

step_by_step.explanation: Higher GL means lower adjustment costs (smooth adjustment hypothesis). Industry C (GL=0.1, mostly inter-industry trade) has highest adjustment costs. Industry B (GL=0.5, mixed) has medium adjustment costs. Industry A (GL=0.9, mostly intra-industry trade) has lowest adjustment costs.
step_by_step.calculation: Highest to lowest adjustment costs: Industry C > Industry B > Industry A.

step_by_step.explanation: Empirical studies suggest: GL>0.8 → wage loss 5-10%; GL 0.4-0.8 → wage loss 10-20%; GL<0.4 → wage loss 20-30%.
step_by_step.calculation:
Industry A (GL=0.9): estimated wage loss ≈ 5-10%
Industry B (GL=0.5): estimated wage loss ≈ 15%
Industry C (GL=0.1): estimated wage loss ≈ 25-30%

step_by_step.explanation: Industry A: minimal intervention needed. Short-term unemployment insurance, job placement services. Most workers will find jobs within industry. Industry B: moderate intervention. Retraining programs, relocation assistance for workers who need to move to other industries or regions. Industry C: intensive intervention. Generous income support, extensive retraining (possibly multi-year), relocation assistance, possibly regional development aid.
step_by_step.calculation:
A: light TAA (UI, placement)
B: moderate TAA (UI, retraining, relocation)
C: intensive TAA (extended UI, multi-year retraining, relocation, regional aid)

step_by_step.step: Part (d): Priority for limited TAA funds
step_by_step.explanation: With limited funds, priority should go to Industry C where adjustment costs are highest and workers are most vulnerable. Industry A workers will adjust without significant assistance. Industry B may need some assistance but less urgent than Industry C.
step_by_step.calculation: Priority: Industry C (highest need) > Industry B > Industry A (lowest need)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Highest adjustment costs: C > B > A; (b) A:5-10%, B:15%, C:25-30% wage loss; (c) A: light TAA, B: moderate TAA, C: intensive TAA; (d) Priority to Industry C (highest adjustment costs).
worked_examples.concept_applied: The smooth adjustment hypothesis predicts that intra-industry trade (high GL) generates lower adjustment costs than inter-industry trade (low GL). Policy interventions should be targeted at low-GL industries where workers face the largest wage losses and longest unemployment spells.

worked_examples.title: Political Economy of Trade Agreements

image.alt: Example analyzing political economy of trade agreements based on IIT
image.caption: Figure 7.3.14: The political feasibility of trade agreements depends on the IIT share — agreements between similar countries (high IIT) face less opposition than agreements between dissimilar countries (low IIT).

worked_examples.problem: Country X is considering three potential trade agreements. With Country Y (similar income, similar factor endowments, close geography). With Country Z (lower income, different endowments, distant). With Country W (similar income, similar endowments, but distant). (a) Rank the agreements from most to least politically feasible. (b) Explain your ranking based on expected IIT and adjustment costs. (c) What compensation mechanisms would be needed for each agreement? (d) How should Country X sequence the agreements if it can only negotiate one at a time?

step_by_step.explanation: Most feasible: Agreement with similar, close country (Y) — highest expected IIT, lowest adjustment costs, least opposition. Next: Agreement with similar but distant country (W) — still high IIT but distance reduces trade volume and IIT somewhat, moderate feasibility. Least feasible: Agreement with dissimilar, distant country (Z) — low IIT, high adjustment costs, strong opposition from import-competing industries.
step_by_step.calculation: Most feasible: Y (similar, close) > W (similar, distant) > Z (dissimilar, distant) — least feasible.

step_by_step.explanation: Country Y: similar endowments and incomes → high IIT (GL ≈ 0.8-0.9) → low adjustment costs → losers are individual firms, not whole industries → less political opposition. Country W: similar endowments but distance reduces trade → moderate IIT (GL ≈ 0.6-0.7) → moderate adjustment costs. Country Z: different endowments → low IIT (GL ≈ 0.2-0.4) → high adjustment costs → entire import-competing industries lose → strong political opposition from industry groups and labor unions.
step_by_step.calculation: IIT drives feasibility: higher IIT → lower adjustment costs → more feasible.

step_by_step.explanation: Agreement Y: minimal compensation needed — possibly worker retraining within industries, firm-level adjustment assistance. Agreement W: moderate compensation — industry-level adjustment programs, regional aid if certain regions are affected. Agreement Z: extensive compensation — generous TAA, multi-year retraining, relocation assistance, agricultural subsidies for farmers (if agriculture is import-competing), possibly side payments to affected regions.
step_by_step.calculation: Y: light compensation; W: moderate compensation; Z: heavy compensation.

step_by_step.explanation: Country X should negotiate the most feasible agreement (Y) first. Success with Y builds political capital and demonstrates benefits of trade liberalization. Then negotiate W, using the experience from Y. Finally, negotiate Z, using the political capital and institutional capacity built from previous agreements. This sequencing also allows time to develop adjustment assistance programs needed for Z.
step_by_step.calculation: Sequence: Y → W → Z (easiest to hardest).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Most feasible: Y > W > Z (least feasible); (b) Y has highest IIT (similar, close), lowest adjustment costs; Z has lowest IIT, highest adjustment costs; (c) Y: light compensation, W: moderate, Z: heavy compensation; (d) Sequence Y first, then W, then Z.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Trade agreements between similar countries (high IIT) face less political opposition and require less compensation than agreements between dissimilar countries (low IIT). Policymakers should sequence agreements from easiest (high IIT) to hardest (low IIT) to build political support.

image.alt: Summary of adjustment costs and policy implications
image.caption: Figure 7.3.15: Summary of adjustment costs and policy implications — high IIT generates low adjustment costs (light TAA), low IIT generates high adjustment costs (heavy TAA). Political feasibility and sequencing of trade agreements depend on IIT levels.

sections.introduction: Intra-industry trade has been increasing globally since the 1960s, driven by falling trade costs, rising incomes, product proliferation, and the fragmentation of production across global value chains. Understanding these trends is essential for analyzing the changing nature of globalization, the declining political opposition to trade in developed countries (except for trade with low-wage countries), and the implications for trade policy in the 21st century.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to trends in intra-industry trade
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.3.16: Intra-industry trade has increased dramatically since 1960, especially between developed countries. The rise of global value chains and product differentiation has driven this trend.

key_concept.definition:
Global trends in intra-industry trade: (1) Overall IIT share for developed countries increased from about 25% in 1960 to over 60% in 2020. (2) IIT is highest between developed countries (60-80% of manufacturing trade) and lowest between developed and developing countries (20-40%). (3) IIT has increased fastest in industries with high R&D intensity (electronics, pharmaceuticals, machinery) and product differentiation. (4) The rise of global value chains has increased measured IIT through vertical specialization — components and finished goods flow in both directions within industries.

Regional patterns: (1) European Union has the highest IIT shares (often >70%) due to deep integration, similar incomes, and geographic proximity. (2) US-Canada IIT increased from 40% to 70% after CUSFTA (1989) and NAFTA (1994). (3) East Asian IIT has increased rapidly as countries like Korea, Taiwan, and China have developed and converged in income with Japan and the US. (4) Developing countries have lower IIT with each other than with developed countries because their income levels are lower and demand for differentiated products is weaker.

Industry trends: (1) IIT is highest in machinery, electronics, chemicals, and transportation equipment (all high-differentiation industries). (2) IIT is lowest in textiles, apparel, footwear, and basic metals (commodity-like or low-differentiation). (3) IIT has increased fastest in electronics and machinery due to global value chains. (4) IIT in agriculture is very low (most agricultural trade is inter-industry) but has increased modestly for processed foods and beverages.

Drivers of increased IIT: (1) Falling trade costs (tariff reductions, transport cost declines, communication technology). (2) Rising incomes (more demand for differentiated products). (3) Product proliferation (more varieties available). (4) Fragmentation of production (global value chains). (5) Trade agreements (EU, NAFTA, WTO).
key_concept.context: The rise of intra-industry trade has been documented extensively. Grubel and Lloyd (1975) found IIT shares of 40-50% for developed countries in the 1960s. By the 1990s, IIT shares had risen to 60-70% (Greenaway and Milner, 1995). More recent studies (Brülhart, 2009; Fontagné and Freudenberg, 1997) confirm continued increases. The rise of global value chains has been particularly important — by 2010, about 50% of global trade was in intermediate goods, much of it intra-industry (Johnson and Noguera, 2012). The COVID-19 pandemic temporarily reduced IIT due to supply chain disruptions, but long-term trends suggest continued growth in IIT as developing countries converge in income and technology. The rise of services trade (digital services, software, financial services) is increasingly characterized by IIT as well.

image.alt: Graph showing trends in intra-industry trade over time
image.caption: Figure 7.3.17: Intra-industry trade shares have increased from 25% in 1960 to over 60% in 2020 for developed countries. The rise is driven by falling trade costs, rising incomes, and global value chains.

categories.description: How IIT varies across regions and trade blocs.

examples.explanation:
The EU has the highest IIT shares in the world, often exceeding 70% for manufacturing trade. Deep integration (single market, common currency for many members), high income levels, geographic proximity, and similar factor endowments drive this high IIT. Newer EU members (Eastern Europe) have lower IIT with Western Europe but are catching up.

Example: Germany-France manufacturing IIT >80%. Germany-Poland IIT ≈60% and rising as Poland converges in income.

US-Canada IIT increased dramatically after CUSFTA (1989) and NAFTA (1994), from about 40% to over 70% for manufacturing. US-Mexico IIT is lower (30-50%) but has increased due to supply chain integration in autos, electronics, and appliances. The USMCA (2020) is expected to further increase IIT.

Example: US-Canada auto IIT >90% (integrated North American auto industry). US-Mexico auto IIT ≈60% (US exports parts, imports assembled vehicles).

East Asian IIT has increased rapidly since the 1990s as Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and China have integrated. The region is characterized by vertical specialization (supply chains) — Japan and Korea export components to China, China assembles and exports final goods to US and Europe. This creates IIT within East Asia and also between East Asia and other regions.

Example: Japan-China IIT in electronics ≈50-60% (Japan exports high-value components, China exports assembled consumer electronics). Korea-China IIT similar.

categories.description: How IIT varies and has changed across industries.

examples.explanation:
Machinery, electronics, transportation equipment, and chemicals have the highest IIT shares (often >60% for developed countries). These industries are characterized by high R&D intensity, product differentiation, economies of scale, and global supply chains. IIT in these industries has increased over time as trade costs fell.

Example: Office machinery GL ≈0.85 (US-EU). Pharmaceuticals GL ≈0.80. Automobiles GL ≈0.85.

Textiles, apparel, footwear, basic metals, and processed foods have lower IIT shares (often <40% for developed countries). These industries are less differentiated, more commodity-like, or have high transport costs relative to value. IIT in these industries has increased modestly but remains low.

Example: Textiles GL ≈0.30 (US-EU). Apparel GL ≈0.25. Basic steel GL ≈0.20.

Electronics and machinery have seen the fastest IIT growth due to global value chains. Components are sourced from multiple countries, assembled elsewhere, and final products exported — all within the same industry classification. This vertical specialization has increased measured IIT even when final products are not highly differentiated.

Example: A smartphone contains components from US (chips), Korea (memory), Japan (display), China (battery, assembly) — all in 'electronics' industry. Trade in components and final phones creates IIT.

categories.description: Factors that have increased IIT over time.

examples.explanation:
Tariffs have fallen from average 40% in 1940s to under 5% in developed countries today. Transport costs have fallen due to containerization, air freight, and logistics improvements. Communication costs have fallen dramatically, enabling coordination of global supply chains. Lower trade costs make it feasible to trade differentiated products and to fragment production across borders.

Example: The cost of shipping a container from Shanghai to Los Angeles fell from \$5,000 in 1980 to \$2,000 in 2020 (in real terms). This made it economical to ship components back and forth within industries.

As incomes rise, consumers demand more variety and higher quality. Firms respond by introducing more product varieties. This increases the scope for intra-industry trade because countries can specialize in different varieties and exchange them. The number of car models, smartphone models, and product SKUs has exploded over time.

Example: In 1970, a typical car buyer had maybe 50 models to choose from. Today, there are over 500 models available in developed markets. This variety expansion is both a cause and a consequence of IIT.

Production fragmentation means that different stages of production occur in different countries. Components flow from developed to developing countries for assembly; finished goods flow back. This creates two-way trade within industries — a country may export components and import finished goods in the same industry, raising measured IIT.

Example: The iPhone is designed in US, chips made in US and Korea, display from Japan, assembly in China, final sale in US and Europe. Trade flows: US exports chips to China, imports assembled iPhones from China — both in 'electronics' industry, creating IIT.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing IIT Trends Over Time

image.alt: Example analyzing IIT trends over time
image.caption: Figure 7.3.18: Analyzing trends in intra-industry trade for a country over a 30-year period.

worked_examples.problem: A country's overall GL index for manufacturing trade increased from 0.35 in 1990 to 0.55 in 2005 to 0.65 in 2020. (a) What does this trend indicate about the country's trade patterns? (b) What factors might explain this increase? (c) How would this trend affect the country's adjustment costs from future trade liberalization? (d) How would this trend affect political support for trade agreements?

step_by_step.explanation: The GL index increased from 0.35 to 0.65, meaning the share of intra-industry trade increased from 35% to 65% of manufacturing trade. This indicates the country is trading more with similar countries (or within industries) and less with dissimilar countries. The country may have become more similar to its trading partners in factor endowments and income, or industries have become more differentiated, or supply chains have fragmented.
step_by_step.calculation: IIT share increased from 35% to 65% over 30 years.

step_by_step.explanation: Possible factors: (1) The country's income per capita increased, making it more similar to developed country partners. (2) Trade costs fell due to trade agreements and technology. (3) The country integrated into global value chains (e.g., joined EU, NAFTA, or WTO). (4) Product differentiation increased in key industries. (5) The country shifted its trade partners from dissimilar to similar countries.
step_by_step.calculation: Rising income, falling trade costs, GVC integration, product differentiation.

step_by_step.explanation: Higher IIT means lower adjustment costs from future trade liberalization. In 1990 (GL=0.35), trade liberalization would have required significant inter-industry adjustment (workers moving across industries). In 2020 (GL=0.65), most trade is intra-industry, so adjustment is within industries — lower costs, faster reemployment, smaller wage losses.
step_by_step.calculation: Adjustment costs decrease as IIT increases.

step_by_step.explanation: Higher IIT increases political support for trade agreements because losers are fewer and easier to compensate. In 1990, trade liberalization would have faced strong opposition from import-competing industries. In 2020, opposition is weaker because trade is mostly intra-industry — losers are individual firms, not whole industries. The country is more likely to support new trade agreements.
step_by_step.calculation: Political support increases as IIT increases.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) IIT share increased from 35% to 65%, indicating more trade with similar countries and more intra-industry trade; (b) Rising income, falling trade costs, GVC integration, product differentiation; (c) Adjustment costs decrease as IIT increases; (d) Political support increases as IIT increases (losers fewer, easier to compensate).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Intra-industry trade has increased globally due to falling trade costs, rising incomes, and global value chains. Higher IIT reduces adjustment costs and increases political support for trade liberalization.

worked_examples.title: Comparing IIT Across Development Levels

image.alt: Example comparing IIT across development levels
image.caption: Figure 7.3.19: Intra-industry trade shares vary systematically with development level — higher income countries have higher IIT, and IIT increases as countries converge in income.

worked_examples.problem: Country A (GDP per capita \$50,000) trades with Country B (GDP per capita \$45,000), Country C (\$20,000), and Country D (\$5,000). (a) Rank the country pairs by expected IIT. (b) If Country C's GDP per capita rises to \$35,000 over 20 years, what happens to IIT between A and C? (c) What does this imply for the relationship between economic development and IIT? (d) How would you expect IIT between two developing countries (both \$5,000) to compare with IIT between a developed and a developing country?

step_by_step.explanation: IIT is highest between countries with similar incomes. A and B have the most similar incomes (\$50k vs \$45k) → highest IIT. A and C have moderate income difference (\$50k vs \$20k) → moderate IIT. A and D have largest income difference (\$50k vs \$5k) → lowest IIT.
step_by_step.calculation: Highest IIT: A-B (similar high incomes) > A-C (moderate difference) > A-D (large difference) — lowest IIT.

step_by_step.explanation: As Country C's income rises from \$20k to \$35k, its income becomes more similar to Country A (\$50k). Demand patterns converge — Country C consumers demand more differentiated products. Also, Country C's factor endowments become more similar to A as capital accumulates. Both effects increase IIT between A and C.
step_by_step.calculation: IIT(A,C) increases as Country C develops and converges in income with A.

step_by_step.explanation: There is a positive relationship between income level and IIT. Richer countries have higher IIT with each other. As countries develop, their IIT with other developed countries increases. This is because richer countries demand more differentiated products and have more similar factor endowments. The Linder hypothesis explains this pattern.
step_by_step.calculation: Higher income → higher IIT (with similar-income partners).

step_by_step.explanation: Two developing countries (both \$5k) have similar incomes, so the Linder hypothesis would predict high IIT between them. However, developing countries have lower demand for differentiated products (they spend more on basic goods) and may lack the industrial capacity to produce many varieties. Empirically, IIT between developing countries is often lower than between developed countries but can be significant for certain industries (e.g., regional trade in Africa, ASEAN). As both develop, their IIT increases.
step_by_step.calculation: Developing-developing IIT is often moderate (30-50%), lower than developed-developed (60-80%) but higher than developed-developing (20-40%) in some cases.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) A-B (highest) > A-C > A-D (lowest); (b) IIT between A and C increases as C's income converges with A; (c) IIT increases with income level and income similarity; (d) Developing-developing IIT is moderate (30-50%), lower than developed-developed but often higher than developed-developing for similar-income pairs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Intra-industry trade is positively related to income level and income similarity. As countries develop and incomes converge, IIT increases. This explains why trade between developed countries is mostly intra-industry while trade between developed and developing countries is mostly inter-industry.

image.alt: Summary of trends and empirical patterns in intra-industry trade
image.caption: Figure 7.3.20: Summary of trends in intra-industry trade — rising global IIT shares, regional patterns (EU highest), industry variation (machinery highest, textiles lowest), and drivers (falling trade costs, rising incomes, global value chains).

summary:
Intra-industry trade (IIT) is the simultaneous export and import of goods within the same industry, while inter-industry trade is the exchange of goods from different industries. Inter-industry trade is explained by comparative advantage (Ricardian model) and factor endowments (Heckscher-Ohlin model) — countries export goods that use their abundant factors intensively and import goods that use their scarce factors. This type of trade dominates between countries with different factor endowments (developed vs developing countries) and generates high adjustment costs because resources must move across industries. Intra-industry trade is explained by economies of scale, product differentiation, and love of variety (Krugman model) — countries both export and import differentiated products within the same industry. This type of trade dominates between countries with similar factor endowments (developed countries) and generates low adjustment costs because resources move within industries.

The Grubel-Lloyd (GL) index measures the share of intra-industry trade: $GL_i = 1 - |X_i - M_i|/(X_i + M_i)$. Values range from 0 (pure inter-industry trade) to 1 (pure intra-industry trade). The overall GL index is the weighted average across industries. Empirical evidence shows that IIT accounts for 60-80% of trade between developed countries but only 20-40% of trade between developed and developing countries. IIT is highest in differentiated industries (automobiles, electronics, pharmaceuticals) and lowest in homogeneous industries (steel, cement, basic chemicals).

The smooth adjustment hypothesis (SAH) states that industries with higher IIT experience lower adjustment costs from trade liberalization. Workers displaced from high-IIT industries find new jobs faster and with smaller wage losses because skills are transferable within the industry. Capital in high-IIT industries can be redeployed within the industry. Regions specialized in high-IIT industries recover faster from trade shocks. This has important policy implications: Trade Adjustment Assistance should be targeted at low-IIT industries where adjustment costs are highest; trade agreements between similar countries (high IIT) face less political opposition and require less compensation; and trade liberalization should be sequenced from high-IIT to low-IIT industries to build political support.

Global trends show that IIT has increased dramatically since 1960, driven by falling trade costs (tariffs, transport, communication), rising incomes (increased demand for differentiated products), product proliferation (more varieties), and the rise of global value chains (vertical specialization). The European Union has the highest IIT shares (>70%), followed by North America (50-70%), and East Asia (40-60%). IIT continues to increase as developing countries converge in income and technology with developed countries. Understanding the distinction between intra-industry and inter-industry trade is essential for analyzing trade patterns, predicting adjustment costs, designing trade policy, and understanding the political economy of globalization.
